author: @jamesraymond.1358

author: @AgentBenzo
text:
Rob... you have the most valuable channel on this platform - AND you have my sincere thanks for teaching me more, in 6 months, than I learned in my 6 years of "higher" education.

If I develop to be even half of the man that you are, that'd be an accomplishment better than all of my previous goals combined.  

I appreciate your contribution ... and have been moved to donate to your cause.

author: @tuseroni6085
text: dont think we get oak galls here in the states, or if we do not where i live, seen a lot oak trees but no galls, suspect the gall wasp never made it here

replies.author: @mikeguitar9769
replies.text: Maybe we could leach the tannic acid out of acorn flour, which also reportedly is the process that makes the flour edible.  Maybe a good project to honor the Native Americans?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you do mate - there are lots of posts from Americans going through this recipe - they don't often explain he chemistry but they do the ink - it must be your area maybe but mike's suggestion is a great one - I don't think any one has tried that - hmmm - going to look for acorns lol

replies.author: @tuseroni6085
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I've looked for em and never seen any in Indiana or Ohio. However I suspect oak bark would work, I have tried when I was tanning some leather using the first pouring batch, but it was just brown, I used rusty nails and lemon juice though.

But oak bark is a good source of tannins.

author: @darrellpidgeon6440
text: Fascinating!  I used to experiment with such things.  I once soaked some crushed green leaves in alcohol.  Days later I would brush it on vellum.  Once dry, the paper had an aged look.  The ink used on U.S. paper money is interesting.  It is possible to pick up the bill with a strong magnet.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is interesting!

author: @SkepticalCaveman
text: Be really careful with iron gall ink if you're using a expensive pen, it will corrode the metal.

author: @SaucemanSauceman
text: I have walnut trees. We make ink snd tincture from the husks.

replies.author: @sarahhoward9081
replies.text: Tincture makes a really good wood stain. Also very good for ringworm. My uncle came to me for help with his dog after 3 months of treatment with high power  antibiotics that actually blinded his dog. I gave him some black walnut tincture and he applied it topically as well is in drinking water. She was cured in a couple weeks. The vet said it was some new "super ringworm" that's resistant to modern treatments. Anyway there's my 2 bits.
